mappings.rationaleAI: Electromagnetic theory through Ampere, Faraday, and Maxwell covers magnetic effects of steady currents (C.2, C.2.a) and electromagnetic induction (C.4). Electron spin and quantum mechanical magnetic properties connect to atomic moments and magnetic resonance (C.3.d). The Earth's magnetic field relates to terrestrial magnetism (C.5.c.vi). Paramagnetism, diamagnetism, and ferromagnetism address classification of magnetic substances (C.5, C.5.c.i-iii). Maxwell's unification connects to electromagnetic theory (E.1.b).

Faraday's discovery of electromagnetic induction directly addresses Faraday's law (B.3.a) and magnetic effects of varying currents (C.4, C.4.a). His influence on Maxwell and Einstein connects to the development of electromagnetic radiation theory (E.1, E.1.a, E.1.b).

Newton's particulate theory and Huygens's wave theory connect to the development of concepts about light (E.1.a). Recognition of light as electromagnetic radiation relates to Maxwell's equations (E.1.b). Wave-particle duality and quantum optics address the quantum theory of radiation (E.2), photoelectric effect (E.2.a), and wave-particle duality of the photon (E.2.b).

Electromagnetic radiation including visible light and wave properties connect to the classical theory of radiation (E.1) and Maxwell's equations (E.1.b). The electromagnetic spectrum directly addresses section E.1.c.

Wave-particle duality and the uncertainty principle connect to the quantum theory of radiation (E.2) and wave-particle duality of the photon (E.2.b). Photoelectric effect evidence relates to E.2.a. Quantum field theory addresses interaction of radiation with atomic structures (E.2.c) and relation to quantum theory and relativity (E.2.d).

Special relativity's connection to the speed of light and mass-energy equivalence relates to the relation of electromagnetic radiation to relativity (E.2.d). General relativity and spacetime connect to relativistic electrodynamics (F) and four-dimensional notation (F.1).
Zero electrical resistance relates to conduction of electricity in metals and semiconductors (B.2.b). The Meissner effect connects to ferromagnetism and related magnetic phenomena (C.5.c.iii).

Ferromagnets and superconductors relate to conduction of electricity (B.2.b). Phase transitions in magnetic materials connect to properties of magnetic materials (C.5) and magnetism of matter (C.5.c).
2,500 years of physics history relates to the development of concepts concerning the nature of light (E.1.a).
Electromagnetism broadly connects to static electric charge (A), moving charges (B), magnetism (C), and the electromagnetic field (E).
Electricity generation relates to AC power generation and transmission (B.3.k) and electromagnetic energy (B.4.c).
Solar photovoltaics connect to piezoelectricity and photoelectric applications (B.5.a), while grid management relates to AC power (B.3.k).
Electromagnetic radiation in the atmosphere connects to electricity in the atmosphere (A.1.e).
Maxwell's field theory and vacuum energy connect to field theory (D, D.1) and Maxwell's equations (E.1.b, E.3.a).
Electron discovery connects to electron emission (B.2.d). Particle physics relates to particle nature of radiation (E.2.a).
NMR spectroscopy connects to magnetic resonance (C.3.d). Conduction mechanisms relate to B.2.b.
Energy conversion connects to thermoelectric effects (B.2.c).
Measurement theory connects to electrostatic measurement (A.4) and AC measurement (B.3.d).
Solar magnetic dynamics connect to terrestrial magnetism (C.5.c.vi).
Earth's magnetic field relates to terrestrial magnetism (C.5.c.vi).
Metal conductivity connects to conduction mechanisms (B.2.b). X-ray fluorescence relates to radiation-matter interaction (E.2.c).
